title: Observable — The Immune Checkpoint Cancer Hijacks

shots.speech_text: Your immune system can recognize and kill a cancer cell. So why doesn't it always? Often, the tumor has found the off-switch — and it's a single molecular handshake.

Neutral seamless 5500K bilateral soft key locked — symmetrical wrap, subtle nose shadow only, zero hue drift, no magenta vignette, no warm/cool lateral split. Locked-off or slow linear track; no handheld. Lip-synced, delivers: "It's called an immune checkpoint. A T cell carries a receptor named PD-1. When PD-1 touches a partner protein, PD-L1, on a neighboring cell, it dials the T cell down — a built-in brake that normally stops your immune system attacking healthy tissue. Many tumors simply coat themselves in PD-L1 and press that brake." clear enthusiastic science communicator voice, precise but accessible diction, synthetic presenter only. Finish on presentational gesture peak, never hold dead stillness or dead frame.
shots.speech_text: It's called an immune checkpoint. A T cell carries a receptor named PD-1. When PD-1 touches a partner protein, PD-L1, on a neighboring cell, it dials the T cell down — a built-in brake that normally stops your immune system attacking healthy tissue. Many tumors simply coat themselves in PD-L1 and press that brake.

shots.speech_text: In twenty fifteen, crystallographers resolved the human PD-1 to PD-L1 contact at two angstroms — coordinates anyone can download. And we know the brake is real because releasing it works: antibodies that block this exact handshake unleash the immune system on tumors, a breakthrough recognized with the twenty-eighteen Nobel Prize in Medicine.
